_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan 完形填空 (academic prose). Ten distinct social-science topics, each passage 240-340 words with 20 numbered blanks, 4 same-class options per blank, exactly one correct by context/discourse logic/collocation and three distractors excluded by context not grammar. DeepSeek not used. answer letters resolve to option index A/B/C/D.

sets.passage: Economists once treated inequality largely as a (1)____ by-product of growth, an unfortunate cost worth paying for rising prosperity. That comfortable assumption has come under (2)____ scrutiny. When the gains of an expanding economy flow (3)____ to those who already own the most, the result is not merely a wider gap in incomes but a slow erosion of opportunity itself. Children born into poverty inherit (4)____ schools, weaker networks, and thinner safety nets, so talent that might have flourished is quietly (5)____. The damage, in other words, is dynamic rather than static. Defenders of steep disparities argue that the prospect of great reward (6)____ effort and innovation, and there is truth in this. Yet beyond a certain point the link (7)____: extreme concentrations of wealth tend to buy political influence, which is then used to (8)____ the very rules that allowed such fortunes to accumulate. Markets that should reward (9)____ begin instead to reward incumbency. The consequences are not only economic. Societies marked by gaping divides report lower levels of trust, poorer health, and more (10)____ politics, as citizens come to (11)____ that the game is rigged against them. None of this implies that perfect equality is either possible or (12)____; differences in effort and luck will always produce differences in outcome. The relevant question is not whether inequality should exist but at what level it becomes (13)____ to the common good. Answering that question requires us to distinguish the inequality that (14)____ from honest contribution from the kind that merely (15)____ advantage across generations. A healthy economy needs incentives, but it also needs the (16)____ belief, widely shared, that hard work can change one's station. When that belief (17)____, the social fabric frays in ways no growth statistic can (18)____. The (19)____ challenge for policy, then, is to preserve the spur of reward while preventing privilege from hardening into a closed and self-perpetuating (20)____.

sets.passage: A public good, in the language of economists, is something that is both non-rival and non-excludable: my enjoyment of clean air or national defence does not (1)____ yours, and no one can easily be shut out. These two features make such goods immensely (2)____, yet they also explain a stubborn puzzle—why so many of them are chronically (3)____. The trouble lies in incentives. Because a person benefits whether or not she contributes, the (4)____ choice for any individual is to let others bear the cost. Economists call this temptation the (5)____ problem. If everyone reasons this way, the good is provided in quantities far (6)____ what the community would actually prefer. Each acts sensibly, and (7)____ all are worse off. The market, so reliable at supplying private goods, here (8)____ to deliver, since no firm can profit from a product it cannot withhold from non-payers. This is why roads, basic research, and disease control are typically financed through (9)____ rather than sales. Yet taxation invites its own (10)____: citizens who cannot see a direct return often resent the bill, and politicians find it easier to fund (11)____ projects than invisible ones. A new stadium is photographed at its opening; the quiet maintenance of a water system, which prevents disasters that never (12)____, wins no such gratitude. The benefits of prevention are real but (13)____, while the costs are immediate and concentrated. Thus public goods suffer a double (14)____: markets will not supply them, and political systems systematically under-reward them. Recognising this pattern does not (15)____ it, but it does point toward remedies—binding commitments, shared rules, and institutions designed to make cooperation the (16)____ path rather than the heroic exception. Above all, it reminds us that some of the things we value most cannot be (17)____ individually. They must be (18)____ collectively or not at all, and a society that forgets this will slowly find its shared foundations (19)____, long before anyone notices the (20)____ has begun.

sets.passage: Few words divide opinion as sharply as globalization. To its (1)____, the integration of markets has lifted hundreds of millions out of poverty, spread technology, and made once-exotic goods affordable to ordinary families. To its critics, the same process has (2)____ communities, depressed wages for the less skilled, and handed extraordinary power to corporations answerable to no electorate. Both stories contain truth, which is precisely why the debate (3)____ unresolved. The classic economic case is elegant. When nations specialise in what they do best and trade for the rest, the total pie (4)____, leaving room for everyone to gain. The flaw lies not in the logic but in the word everyone. Theory shows that the winners gain enough to (5)____ the losers in principle; it does not guarantee that they actually (6)____. A factory town whose jobs migrate overseas experiences the loss as (7)____ and personal, while the diffuse benefit—slightly cheaper products for distant consumers—is barely (8)____. The gains are real but spread thin; the pain is sharp and (9)____. Critics also point to a deeper (10)____: as capital crosses borders freely while labour and democratic institutions remain (11)____ tied to nations, governments compete to attract investment by (12)____ taxes, weakening protections, and asking workers to accept less. The race, they warn, runs toward the (13)____. Defenders reply that retreat into protectionism would (14)____ the poor it claims to help and invite the very stagnation it fears. The more honest conclusion is that globalization is neither a (15)____ nor a curse but a powerful tool whose effects depend on the rules that (16)____ it and the cushions a society (17)____ for those it displaces. The failure has often been less in the trade itself than in the (18)____ of any serious plan to share its gains and ease its losses. To reject openness outright is to (19)____ the baby with the bathwater; to embrace it uncritically is to (20)____ the bath is empty.

sets.passage: What is a university actually for? The question sounds naive, yet the answers a society gives quietly (1)____ how it spends billions and shapes generations. One familiar reply is economic: higher education exists to (2)____ workers with marketable skills, raising both their wages and national productivity. On this view, a degree is essentially an (3)____ that pays a measurable return, and a course of study should be judged by the salaries it (4)____. There is obvious sense here, but taken alone it leaves much (5)____. If job training were the whole purpose, much of the curriculum—history, philosophy, the close reading of poems—would appear merely (6)____. A rival tradition insists that the deeper aim is the (7)____ of judgement: to teach students not what to think but how, equipping them to weigh evidence, tolerate doubt, and revise their beliefs when the facts (8)____. Such capacities rarely appear on a payslip, yet a democracy starved of them grows dangerously (9)____ to manipulation. A third answer is frankly social. Universities sort and certify, acting less as engines of learning than as elaborate (10)____ that signal who is diligent and able. On this cynical reading, much of what students gain is not knowledge but a (11)____ that opens doors—a function that would survive even if little were truly (12)____. These purposes need not (13)____ one another, but they pull in different directions, and confusion about which (14)____ leads to incoherent policy. A system that promises career returns while delivering broad culture will (15)____ students expecting jobs; one that abandons the wider mission to chase employment statistics may (16)____ its soul to balance its books. The wisest institutions resist the pressure to (17)____ themselves to a single metric. They recognise that the most (18)____ outcomes of education—curiosity, integrity, the courage to think independently—are precisely the ones that (19)____ measurement, and that a culture which funds only what it can count will, in time, find itself unable to (20)____ what it has lost.

sets.passage: Markets are usually praised, or condemned, for their efficiency—their genius at matching what people want with what others can supply. Less often noticed is a quieter effect: markets do not merely (1)____ our preferences, they slowly (2)____ them. When more and more of life is organised around buying and selling, the very language we use to value things begins to (3)____. Consider what happens when a good once exchanged as a gift, a favour, or a duty is suddenly (4)____ a price. Blood donation offers a famous example. Where giving blood is treated as an act of civic (5)____, supply often holds up; introduce cash payment, and some donors, feeling their gift has been (6)____ into a mere transaction, simply (7)____ away. The market intended to expand the supply can perversely (8)____ it, because it changes what the act (9)____. This is the deeper worry the critics raise. They do not deny that trade brings prosperity; they fear that the relentless spread of market norms can (10)____ out other norms—the loyalty of friends, the dedication of teachers, the impartial duty of public servants—that money cannot buy and may quietly (11)____. Once we are accustomed to pricing everything, we risk losing the (12)____ to recognise that some things should not be for sale at all. Defenders answer, reasonably, that markets are merely (13)____, indifferent to ends, and that the fault lies with us if we misuse them. Yet tools are not always so (14)____; the habit of reducing every question to cost and benefit can subtly (15)____ how we see one another, encouraging us to treat people as means rather than (16)____. The point is not to (17)____ markets, which remain among humanity's most useful inventions, but to keep them in their (18)____. A society that prices everything may end up (19)____ nothing, having quietly traded away the very (20)____ that money was supposed to serve.
sets.explain_zh: 1satisfy市场不只满足偏好 2reshape更慢慢重塑偏好 3shift估价的语言开始转变 4given被标上价格(give a price) 5duty公民义务之举 6cheapened馈赠被贬为交易 7drift一些人悄然离开(drift away) 8shrink反而使供给萎缩 9means改变了行为的含义 10crowd把其他规范挤出(crowd out) 11corrode金钱可能悄然腐蚀 12capacity失去辨识的能力 13neutral市场只是中性的 14innocent工具未必那么无辜 15warp扭曲我们看待彼此的方式 16ends把人当手段而非目的 17abolish重点不是废除市场 18place让其各安其位(in their place) 19valuing定价一切者终将一无所重(value) 20values换走了金钱本应服务的价值。

sets.passage: For most of the twentieth century, the wealth of a nation was judged by a single famous number: Gross Domestic Product, the total value of goods and services it produced. GDP is a powerful (1)____, and its rise broadly tracks longer lives and fuller stomachs. Yet a growing chorus of economists now argues that the measure, taken as a (2)____ of national success, is dangerously (3)____. The trouble is what it leaves (4)____. GDP counts a forest only when it is cut down and sold, not the clean air and shelter it (5)____ while standing. It records the money spent treating illness or repairing storm damage as (6)____, as though sickness and disaster were forms of progress. Meanwhile, work that sustains every society—a parent raising children, a neighbour caring for the elderly—goes (7)____ because no money changes hands. The number, in short, is (8)____ to the difference between activity that builds wellbeing and activity that merely (9)____ harm. Critics also note that an average can (10)____ as much as it reveals: a country's GDP per head may climb impressively even as most of its citizens grow no (11)____ off, if the gains pool at the very top. What we measure, the reformers insist, quietly shapes what we (12)____. A government judged solely by output will chase growth even when it (13)____ the very things—health, leisure, a livable environment—that make prosperity worth having. The response has not been to (14)____ GDP but to (15)____ it with broader indicators: measures of health, education, inequality, and even self-reported satisfaction. Such dashboards are admittedly (16)____ to compile and easy to dispute, since happiness resists the tidy precision of a ledger. But the deeper lesson is (17)____. No single figure can (18)____ the texture of a good life, and a society that mistakes its instruments for its (19)____ will steer confidently toward the wrong (20)____.
sets.explain_zh: 1indicator GDP是有力的指标 2measure作为成功的衡量标准 3incomplete危险地不完整 4out问题在于它遗漏的(leaves out) 5provides森林矗立时提供的清洁空气 6gain把治病修灾的支出记为增益 7uncounted无偿劳动未被计入 8blind对二者差别视而不见 9repairs仅修补损害的活动 10conceal平均数掩盖的与揭示的一样多 11better多数人并未更富 12value度量什么便悄然塑造珍视什么 13destroys即便毁掉健康等也追逐增长 14abandon并非抛弃GDP 15supplement而是以更广指标补充 16messy这类仪表盘编制起来杂乱 17plain更深的教训很朴素 18capture没有单一数字能捕捉好生活的肌理 19goals把工具误作目标 20destination自信驶向错误的目的地。

sets.passage: Why does any society bother to support its artists? In strictly practical terms, a poem feeds no one and a symphony shelters no family, and yet no human culture, however poor, has ever done (1)____ art. The persistence of this apparent luxury suggests it answers a need we do not always (2)____ name. One common defence is economic: museums draw tourists, films employ thousands, and a lively cultural scene helps a city (3)____ talent. These arguments are true but (4)____, for they justify art only by reference to something else, treating beauty as a means to fuller hotels. A more ambitious claim is that art (5)____ us to inhabit lives not our own. A novel can place a reader inside the mind of a stranger across centuries or continents, and in doing so it quietly (6)____ the muscles of empathy that a narrow life leaves (7)____. Art, on this view, is a kind of moral (8)____, expanding the circle of those whose suffering we can (9)____ imagine. Others stress a different power. Where ordinary language hurries toward the useful, art (10)____ us, forcing attention onto the texture of experience we normally rush (11)____. A painting can make us see a face we have looked at a thousand times as if for the (12)____ time. Authoritarian regimes grasp this instinctively, which is why they so often (13)____ to control or silence artists: a society that can imagine alternatives is harder to (14)____. None of this means art must be earnest or improving; much of it exists simply to (15)____, and delight is no small thing in a hard world. But the deeper case is that a culture without art grows (16)____, capable of building and trading yet unable to (17)____ what any of it is for. The artist, by this account, is not a (18)____ on a serious society but one of the instruments by which a people (19)____ on who they are and dares to imagine who they might (20)____ become.
sets.explain_zh: 1without从未有文化没有艺术(do without) 2know回应我们未必能道出的需求 3attract帮助城市吸引人才 4thin论证为真却单薄 5allows艺术让我们栖居他者人生 6exercises锻炼共情之肌 7unused狭窄人生使之闲置 8education一种道德的教育 9vividly能生动想象其苦 10slows艺术使我们慢下来 11past我们平时匆匆掠过(rush past) 12first仿佛初次看见 13seek威权常设法控制艺术家 14rule能想象替代方案者更难被统治 15delight许多艺术只为愉悦 16narrower无艺术的文化变得狭隘 17say说不出这一切为何 18burden艺术家不是负担(burden on) 19reflects民族借以反思自身 20yet敢想象将来可能成为谁。

sets.passage: The idea of progress—that humanity is, on the whole, moving toward something better—shapes the modern mind so deeply that we rarely (1)____ it. Yet for most of recorded history the assumption ran the other way. Many ancient cultures believed the world was in slow (2)____ from a vanished golden age, and looked backward for wisdom rather than (3)____ for improvement. The confident forward gaze is a comparatively (4)____ invention, born with the scientific revolution and the dazzling growth it (5)____. There is much to (6)____ the optimists. Across centuries, life has lengthened, plagues have been tamed, and ideas once thought (7)____—that slavery is wrong, that ordinary people deserve a voice—have spread across the globe. To deny all this would be wilfully (8)____. And yet the faith in progress conceals two (9)____ traps. The first is to assume that advance is (10)____, a law of history rather than the hard-won fruit of choices that could be (11)____. Hopes of permanent peace died in the trenches of the last century, a reminder that gains can be (12)____ as well as made. The second trap is subtler: to assume that progress in one domain (13)____ progress in all. Our technical powers have grown astonishingly, yet it is far from clear that we have grown proportionately (14)____ in using them, and the same science that conquers disease can also (15)____ weapons of unprecedented reach. Material advance, in short, does not automatically (16)____ moral advance, and to confuse the two is to mistake speed for (17)____. A mature idea of progress, then, neither despairs nor (18)____ complacent. It holds that things can get better, that they often have, but that nothing (19)____ they will continue to do so. Improvement, on this view, is not a current that carries us along whether we paddle or not; it is a direction we must (20)____ choose, and can always abandon.
sets.explain_zh: 1question我们很少质疑进步观 2decline认为世界从黄金时代缓慢衰落 3forward而非向前求改进(与backward对) 4recent前瞻自信是较晚近的发明 5unleashed科学释放的惊人增长 6encourage有许多支持乐观者(与optimists搭) 7radical曾被视为激进的观念 8blind否认这一切是故意视而不见 9dangerous隐藏两个危险陷阱 10inevitable误以为进步是必然的 11reversed本可被逆转的选择 12lost进步可得亦可失(lost与made对) 13guarantees误以为一域之进步保证全域 14wiser未必相应更有智慧 15forge同一科学也能锻造武器 16ensure物质进步不自动确保道德进步 17direction把速度误作方向 18stays既不绝望也不安于自满(stays complacent) 19guarantees无物保证进步将持续 20actively是须主动选择的方向。

sets.passage: When the biologist Garrett Hardin described the "tragedy of the commons," he painted a bleak picture. Imagine a pasture open to all herders. Each gains the full benefit of adding one more animal, while the cost of overgrazing is (1)____ among everyone. So each rationally adds beasts until the field is (2)____ and ruined. The grim moral seemed to be that shared resources are doomed unless (3)____ privatised or seized by the state. For decades this parable was treated as an iron (4)____. Then the political scientist Elinor Ostrom did something Hardin had not: she went out and (5)____ how real communities actually manage shared forests, fisheries, and irrigation systems. What she found (6)____ the gloomy theory. Around the world, ordinary people had devised (7)____ arrangements that sustained common resources for centuries without either private ownership or distant rulers. The herders, it turned out, were not the helpless prisoners of their own (8)____ that the model assumed. Ostrom identified the conditions under which cooperation (9)____. Successful commons tended to have clear (10)____ about who could use the resource, rules crafted by the users themselves rather than (11)____ from above, and cheap ways to (12)____ on one another so that cheating was quickly noticed. Crucially, there were graduated (13)____ for those who broke the rules—not ruinous, but enough to (14)____ the temptation to free-ride. None of this happened automatically; cooperation had to be patiently (15)____ and could collapse if trust eroded. The lesson is not that markets and governments are (16)____, but that they are not the only tools available. Between the pure market and the heavy state lies a vast, often (17)____ territory of self-organisation, in which communities solve collective problems by (18)____ the very rules they then agree to (19)____. Human beings, Ostrom showed, are neither saints nor the pure egoists of the textbook, but creatures fully capable of (20)____ when the conditions are right.
sets.explain_zh: 1shared过度放牧的成本由众人分摊 2exhausted田地被耗尽毁坏 3either除非或私有或国有(either...or) 4law被当作铁律 5studied她出去实地研究 6complicated研究使悲观理论复杂化(反驳) 7ingenious人们设计出巧妙安排 8logic并非自身逻辑的囚徒 9succeeds合作得以成功的条件 10boundaries清晰的边界(谁可用) 11imposed规则由使用者自定而非自上强加 12monitor廉价地相互监督 13penalties对违规者的分级惩罚 14curb足以抑制搭便车诱惑 15built合作须耐心构建 16useless并非市场政府无用 17overlooked常被忽视的自组织领域 18crafting通过自行制定规则 19obey然后同意遵守 20cooperation条件合适时完全能够合作。

sets.passage: Trust is the invisible currency on which complex societies (1)____. We hand our money to banks we never inspect, swallow pills tested by people we will never meet, and board planes flown by strangers, all because we (2)____ the institutions behind them to do their jobs. When such trust is high, life runs smoothly and cheaply; when it (3)____, every transaction must be guarded with contracts, audits, and suspicion, and the friction (4)____ everything down. It is alarming, then, that confidence in institutions—governments, media, courts, even science—has been visibly (5)____ across many democracies. Diagnosing the cause is not simple. Some blame a (6)____ of genuine failures: scandals, broken promises, and crises that institutions handled (7)____, giving citizens real reason for doubt. On this account, falling trust is a (8)____ response to poor performance, and the remedy is for institutions to earn confidence back by behaving (9)____. Others point to the changed information landscape, in which every misstep is instantly (10)____ and amplified, while a fragmented media rewards outrage over (11)____ reporting. Trust, they argue, has not necessarily fallen because institutions grew worse, but because their flaws became more (12)____. Both factors are probably at work, and they (13)____ each other in a vicious circle. The danger is that distrust, once entrenched, becomes self-(14)____: citizens who assume the worst stop cooperating, comply only when watched, and elect leaders who promise to burn the system down, thereby (15)____ the very dysfunction they feared. Yet blind trust is no remedy either, for institutions unchecked by scepticism tend to (16)____. The healthy condition is not faith but warranted confidence—trust that is (17)____ on evidence and withdrawn when that evidence (18)____. Rebuilding it is slow, unglamorous work: transparency, accountability, and the patient keeping of small promises. Trust, after all, arrives on foot and departs on horseback, and a society that lets it (19)____ may take generations to (20)____ what was lost.
